request.question: Heavyweights and The Santa Clause 2, share which genres?

title: The Santa Clause 2
David Krumholtz
Heavyweights
The Santa Clause (film series)
Liliana Mumy
The Santa Clause 3: The Escape Clause
Greatest Time of Year

sentences: The Santa Clause 2 (alternatively known as The Santa Clause 2: Mrs. Clause) is a 2002 American Christmas romantic comedy-drama film directed by Michael Lembeck.
It is the second installment in "The Santa Clause trilogy", following "The Santa Clause".
It was filmed in the Canadian cities of Vancouver and Calgary.
All the principal actors from the first film reprise their roles.
David Krumholtz (born May 15, 1978) is an American actor.
He played Charlie Eppes in the CBS drama series "Numbers".
He played Seth Goldstein in "Harold & Kumar Go to White Castle" and its two sequels, "Harold & Kumar Escape from Guantanamo Bay" and "A Very Harold & Kumar 3D Christmas".
He is also known for his role as Bernard the Elf in "The Santa Clause" and its sequel, "The Santa Clause 2".
Additionally, he is known for his role as Mr. Universe in the 2005 film "Serenity" and played Michael the AV geek in "10 Things I Hate About You".
In 2016, he provided the voice for Kareem Abdul Lavash in the adult animated film "Sausage Party".
Heavyweights is a 1995 American comedy-drama film directed by Steven Brill and written by Brill with Judd Apatow.
The film centers around a fat camp for kids that is taken over by a fitness guru named Tony Perkis (Ben Stiller).
The Santa Clause is a series of comedy films starring Tim Allen.
The film series began with "The Santa Clause" (1994).
It was followed by "The Santa Clause 2" (2002) and "" (2006).
The series experienced a diminishing critical reception with each subsequent film.
Liliana Berry Davis Mumy (born April 16, 1994) is an American actress and voice actress.

She appeared as Jessica Baker in the "Cheaper by the Dozen" movies with Steve Martin, Lucy Miller in "The Santa Clause 2" and "" with Tim Allen, and was the voice of Mertle Edmonds in the "Lilo & Stitch" franchise's seq films and "".
The Santa Clause 3: The Escape Clause is a 2006 American Christmas fantasy comedy adventure film directed by Michael Lembeck.
It is the third and final installment in "The Santa Clause" trilogy following "The Santa Clause" and "The Santa Clause 2".
"Greatest Time of Year" is the only single from Aly & AJ's Christmas album "Acoustic Hearts of Winter".
The single was used for "", similar to the way Hilary Duff's song "Santa Clause Lane" was used for "The Santa Clause 2", and the music video also had scenes from the film.
The video first aired on October 16, 2006 on the Disney Channel.
The single debuted at number 96 on the US "Billboard" Hot 100 chart.
Don Rhymer (February 23, 1961 – November 28, 2012) was an American screenwriter and film producer.
He graduated from James Madison University in 1982.
He wrote movies like "Big Momma's House", "The Santa Clause 2", "", "The Honeymooners", "Deck the Halls", and the computer animated mockumentary "Surf's Up".
The Santa Clause is a 1994 American Christmas fantasy family comedy film directed by John Pasquin.
It is the first installment in "The Santa Clause trilogy" and it stars Tim Allen as Scott Calvin, an ordinary man who accidentally causes Santa Claus to fall from his roof on Christmas Eve.

When he and his young son, Charlie, finish St. Nick's trip and deliveries, they go to the North Pole where Scott learns that he must become the new Santa and convince those he loves that he is indeed Father Christmas.

The Supremacy Clause of the United States Constitution (Article VI, Clause 2) establishes that the Constitution, federal laws made pursuant to it, and treaties made under its authority, constitute the supreme law of the land.
It provides that state courts are bound by the supreme law; in case of conflict between federal and state law, the federal law must be applied.
Even state constitutions are subordinate to federal law.
In essence, it is a conflict-of-laws rule specifying that certain national acts take priority over any state acts that conflict with national law.

In this respect, the Supremacy Clause follows the lead of Article XIII of the Articles of Confederation, which provided that "Every State shall abide by the determination of the United States in Congress Assembled, on all questions which by this confederation are submitted to them."

A constitutional provision announcing the supremacy of federal law, the Supremacy Clause assumes the underlying priority of federal authority, at least when that authority is expressed in the Constitution itself.
No matter what the federal government or the states might wish to do, they have to stay within the boundaries of the Constitution.
This makes the Supremacy Clause the cornerstone of the whole American political structure.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question` and `passages`, produce the single field `summary`.

Goal: compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a short intermediate state that makes it easy for the next-hop agent to (a) see what facts are present per candidate/entity, (b) see what is ambiguous or missing, and (c) know 1–4 prioritized next-hop retrieval targets or concise queries. This module does not produce the final answer unless the provided passages already unambiguously contain every fact required to resolve the question's relation for every argument.

Procedure:
1) Decide whether the question's relation is semantically complete given the supplied passages. Treat the hop as INCOMPLETE if any of the following hold: a) the relation requires combining or comparing facts across pages (e.g., comparing birth dates, publication/founding years, ordinal ranks, confirming a named director among candidates) and the passages do not supply each required fact unambiguously for all relevant arguments; b) any relation argument is ambiguous or has multiple candidate pages or mentions in the passages; c) an expected candidate page or explicit fact for an argument is missing (e.g., only one side of a comparison is present). Do not trigger INCOMPLETE solely from surface tokens (years, digits) — base the decision on whether the relation's arguments are unambiguously resolved.

2) If the hop is COMPLETE (all required facts to resolve the relation for every argument are present unambiguously in the passages): produce a concise derived result in `summary`. This may be a short phrase or sentence that states the necessary fact(s) needed by the pipeline. Keep it minimal and grounded in the passages; you may include a 1–2 token citation (passage title) if helpful.

3) If the hop is INCOMPLETE or AMBIGUOUS: do NOT assert a final answer or perform the final comparison/selection. Instead emit a short structured report in `summary` containing these labeled elements (prefer bullets or a compact paragraph with explicit labels). Keep all text neutral and non-assertive.

  - evidence: For each named candidate/argument mentioned in the question or passages, list the candidate label and the exact passage title(s) that mention it plus a very short quoted snippet or explicit fact token found there (1–12 words). If a candidate is not mentioned, say so briefly.

  - ambiguities_or_missing: Explicitly name any ambiguities (e.g., multiple candidates for the same name, multiple pages that could be the intended entity) and any missing expected pages or facts.

  - missing_facts_or_bridge: State exactly which fact(s) or disambiguation is needed to complete the relation (e.g., "birth date for Person A", "publication year of Work B", "director credit for Film C"). Be as specific and minimal as possible.

  - retrieval_direction: Provide 1–4 prioritized next-hop targets or concise queries to resolve the missing facts/ambiguities. Prefer explicit page titles when available (exact if known), otherwise provide short search-style queries (few words) or a clear description of the document to retrieve. Order targets by priority.

Formatting and style constraints (apply to both COMPLETE and INCOMPLETE cases):
- The output must be a single `summary` field only. Do not emit other fields.
- In INCOMPLETE reports, use neutral, non-assertive phrasing. Do not produce final comparisons, numeric answers, or definitive selections (e.g., avoid "X is the oldest" or "Answer: 3").
- Keep the report compact and actionable (short labeled bullets or one concise paragraph). Avoid long prose.
- It is allowed and encouraged to quote short factual snippets from passages and cite passage titles to show grounding.
- Do not defer or mark INCOMPLETE when the passages already unambiguously provide all facts for the relation and its arguments.

Produce the `summary` content that follows these rules.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the single field `query`.

Goal
- Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-intent query suitable for a second-hop search that will recover the missing bridging evidence needed to answer the question. Do not produce the final answer in `query`.

Semantic decision rule (when to emit a retrieval-intent query)
- If summary_1 does NOT assert a confirmed linking entity or verified bridging fact needed to answer the question, you MUST emit exactly one compact retrieval-intent query. "Does not assert a confirmed bridge" includes short or candidate-only summaries (e.g., a single named entity, a quoted candidate title, or numeric anchors that are not verified) and any case where the visible anchors do not unambiguously identify the needed bridge.
- If summary_1 already names a confirmed bridge (a clearly identified, verified linking entity or unambiguous metadata that directly connects the question's pieces), you may produce a compact query to fetch supporting evidence, but do NOT produce an answer in the `query` field.
- If summary_1 explicitly states that the bridge cannot be recovered automatically (extreme ambiguity or user-local context requiring clarification), do NOT force a misleading retrieval query. In that case, set `query` to the single short token BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE (all caps, no punctuation) so downstream steps can prompt the user for clarification. Do not invent facts.

Query construction rules
- Output exactly one compact line of text as `query`. Keep it short and retrieval-focused (few keywords, no full sentences). Use visible anchors from `question` and `summary_1` and minimal retrieval keywords that express the missing relation (e.g., anchor1 anchor2 which artist recorded both; AlbumTitle album artist genre; 1991–92 NHL season regular season).
- Do not include the final answer, assertions, or user-facing instructions in the `query` field. Avoid polite phrasing, questions to the user, or explanatory text. Prefer noun phrases and relation keywords.
- If your natural impulse would be to write a declarative answer or a clarification sentence in `query`, instead convert that output into a compact retrieval-intent query that targets the missing bridge.
- Preserve semantic fidelity: base the query only on visible anchors and relations in `question` and `summary_1`. Do not invent hidden identifiers or assume unseen facts.

Format contract
- Return exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short string. If unrecoverable as described above, set `query` to BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Examples of disallowed outputs for `query`
- A declarative answer (e.g., "The artist was X.")
- A user-facing clarification sentence (e.g., "Do you mean X or Y?") unless summary_1 explicitly requires clarification — in that case use BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Behavioral constraints
- Do not use surface heuristics (token count, punctuation, or quotation marks) as the trigger; apply the semantic rule above.
- Emit only one compact retrieval string when the bridge is missing; do not emit multiple queries or long explanations.

Inputs available: `question`, `summary_1`. Output required: single field `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`. The `summary` must be a concise, post-retrieval integration of the evidence that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Follow these rules when composing `summary`:

- Purpose: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` into a grounded state that retains or resolves the earlier bridge only when the second-hop passages supply relation- or attribute-facts that are necessary to answer or disambiguate the `question` (for example: partnership/membership links, joint-award partners indicated by 'with', or requested biographical qualifiers such as nationality, birthdate, current team/status).

- When to append supporting sentence(s): Only append short passage-grounded supporting sentence(s) when the question's semantic intent requires relation or attribute facts to answer or disambiguate. In those cases, produce a compact integrated summary that (a) preserves the concise primary answer or canonical label/token chosen from `context`, and (b) appends one short supporting sentence per relevant entity (or one joint sentence for explicit partnerships) that is directly supported by the supplied `passages` and identity-aligned to the entity referenced in `context`.

- When not to append: Do not append supporting sentences and instead return only the canonical label/token when any of the following apply: the question expects a normalized identifier or single token (examples: binary/multiple-choice label selection between named options, identification-only questions, or explicit token-extraction such as 'middle name'); or the `context` already contains a full, passage-consistent, multi-clause integrated answer. These are semantic exceptions — do not append merely because an answer in `context` is short.

- Identity alignment and verification: Before appending, confirm that the fact in `passages` refers to the same entity referenced in `context` (match names, common aliases, roles, dates, or other corroborating attributes). If identity is ambiguous or unsupported, refrain from appending and preserve uncertainty instead.

- Style and content constraints: Keep output concise. If appending, limit `summary` to 1–3 sentences total (the primary concise answer plus at most one short supporting sentence per relevant entity or one joint supporting sentence). Avoid inventing facts, avoid long verbatim quotations from `passages`, and avoid name-collision errors. Do not change which label or name is selected by the existing answer-selection logic.

- If there is insufficient information in `context` and `passages` to resolve the needed relation/attribute, summarize the uncertainty succinctly rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Produce only the `summary` field as the integrated output string.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use only summary_1 and summary_2 as evidence. Treat summary_2 as the later, integrated evidence and prioritize it when reconciling information; use summary_1 only as the earlier, pre-second-hop context. Do not assume the final state is unresolved solely because summary_1 contains bridging or retrieval-oriented language.

Produce exactly one field, answer, whose value is the minimal final answer string that is fully supported by the visible summaries. The answer field must contain only that minimal plain-text string and nothing else (no explanation, no surrounding quotes, no JSON wrappers, no debugging or transport artifacts).

Decide whether the question's intent is binary (a truth-value / yes-no question) or non-binary (asking for a name, title, date, place, numeric count, selection, or other factual value). Make this decision semantically (based on the question's meaning), not by surface heuristics like token counts or punctuation.

- If the question's intent is binary AND both summary_1 and summary_2 unambiguously agree on the same truth-value, emit exactly one lowercase token: either yes or no (and nothing else) as the answer value.

- For all other question intents (non-binary), emit the minimal factual string supported by the summaries exactly as needed: preserve multi-token values, necessary punctuation, full date formats, and place formats (for example, full names, "City, State", or "Month Day, Year"), and do not truncate these to a single token.

If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not jointly support an unambiguous minimal answer, do not fabricate or over-assert; follow the system's established fallback behavior for ambiguous or unsupported questions.

Always forbid emitting serialized queries, JSON/dict-like structures, internal-debug wrappers, or other transport artifacts as the final answer. The answer must be plain text only.

Make all judgments about minimality and agreement based on the semantic content of summary_1 and summary_2, with priority to summary_2 when they differ.

Produce the answer value exactly as specified above in the answer field.

response.hop1_query: Heavyweights and The Santa Clause 2, share which genres?
hop1_docs: Santa Claus Is Comin' to Town (film) | Santa Claus Is Comin' to Town is a 1970 Christmas stop motion animated television special produced by Rankin/Bass Productions.  The film stars Fred Astaire as the narrator S.D. Kluger, Mickey Rooney as Kris Kringle/Santa Claus, Keenan Wynn as the Winter Warlock, and Paul Frees in various roles.  The film tells the story of how Santa Claus and several Claus-related Christmas traditions came to be.  It is based on the hit Christmas song "Santa Claus Is Comin' to Town", which was introduced on radio by Eddie Cantor in 1934, and the story of Saint Nicholas.

Santa Claus in film | Motion pictures featuring Santa Claus abound and apparently constitute their own subgenre of the Christmas film genre.  Early films of Santa revolve around similar simple plots of Santa's Christmas Eve visit to children.  In 1897, in a short film called "Santa Claus Filling Stockings", Santa Claus is simply filling stockings from his pack of toys.  Another film called "Santa Claus and the Children" was made in 1898.  A year later, a film directed by George Albert Smith in titled "Santa Claus" (or "The Visit from Santa Claus" in the United Kingdom) was created.  In this picture, Santa Claus enters the room from the fireplace and proceeds to trim the tree.  He then fills the stockings that were previously hung on the mantle by the children.  After walking backward and surveying his work, he suddenly darts at the fireplace and disappears up the chimney.  "Santa Claus' Visit" in 1900 featured a scene with two little children kneeling at the feet of their mother and saying their prayers.  The mother tucks the children snugly in bed and leaves the room.  Santa Claus suddenly appears on the roof, just outside the children's bedroom window, and proceeds to enter the chimney, taking with him his bag of presents and a little hand sled for one of the children.  He goes down the chimney and suddenly appears in the children's room through the fireplace.  He distributes the presents and mysteriously causes the appearance of a Christmas tree laden with gifts.  The scene closes with the children waking up and running to the fireplace just too late to catch him by the legs.  A 1909 film by D. W. Griffith titled "A Trap for Santa Claus" shows children setting a trap to capture Santa Claus as he descends the chimney, but instead capture their father who abandoned them and their mother but tries to burglarize the house after he discovers she inherited a fortune.  A twenty-nine-minute 1925 silent film production titled "Santa Claus", by explorer/documentarian Frank E. Kleinschmidt, filmed partly in northern Alaska, feature Santa in his workshop, visiting his Eskimo neighbors, and tending his reindeer.  A year later, another movie titled "Santa Claus" was produced with sound on De Forest Phonofilm.  Over the years, various actors have donned the red suit (aside from those discussed below), including Monty Woolley in "Life Begins at Eight-thirty" (1942), Alberto Rabagliati in "The Christmas That Almost Wasn't" (1966), Dan Aykroyd in "Trading Places" (1983), Jan Rubes in "One Magic Christmas" (1985), David Huddleston in "" (1985), Jonathan Taylor Thomas in "I'll Be Home for Christmas" (1998), and Ed Asner in "Elf" (2003).  Later films about Santa vary, but can be divided into the following themes.

The Santa Clause 3: The Escape Clause | The Santa Clause 3: The Escape Clause is a 2006 American Christmas fantasy comedy adventure film directed by Michael Lembeck.  It is the third and final installment in "The Santa Clause" trilogy following "The Santa Clause" and "The Santa Clause 2".

The Year Without a Santa Claus (2006 film) | The Year Without a Santa Claus is a 90-minute 2006 live-action remake of the Rankin-Bass classic "The Year Without a Santa Claus" which premiered on NBC December 11, 2006.  A widescreen DVD was released on December 12, 2006 (UPC 085391115120).

The Santa Clause 2 | The Santa Clause 2 (alternatively known as The Santa Clause 2: Mrs. Clause) is a 2002 American Christmas romantic comedy-drama film directed by Michael Lembeck.  It is the second installment in "The Santa Clause trilogy", following "The Santa Clause".  It was filmed in the Canadian cities of Vancouver and Calgary.  All the principal actors from the first film reprise their roles.

Santa Claus Park | Santa Claus Park is an attraction being developed near the community of Santa Claus, Indiana.  In 2005, a local development company purchased Santa's Candy Castle and other buildings that comprised Santa Claus Town and announced plans to restore and re-open them to the public.  Santa's Candy Castle was the first building of the original Santa Claus Town to be re-opened to the public, when its doors opened on July 1, 2006.  The 40-ton, 22-foot concrete Santa Claus statue was restored in 2011.  In 2012, a local historic church and the town's original post office were moved to the site next to the large Santa Claus statue.

The Santa Clause (film series) | The Santa Clause is a series of comedy films starring Tim Allen.  The film series began with "The Santa Clause" (1994).  It was followed by "The Santa Clause 2" (2002) and "" (2006).  The series experienced a diminishing critical reception with each subsequent film.
hop1_titles: Santa Claus Is Comin' to Town (film)
The Santa Clause 3: The Escape Clause
The Year Without a Santa Claus (2006 film)
response.summary_1:
- evidence:
  - Heavyweights: NOT present in provided passages (no page or snippet).
  - The Santa Clause 2 (passage 5): "2002 American Christmas romantic comedy-drama film" (snippet).

- ambiguities_or_missing:
  - Heavyweights page and genre information are missing.
  - The Santa Clause 2 uses a compound label "romantic comedy-drama" and also lists "Christmas" — unclear whether to treat these as separate genres (romance, comedy, drama, Christmas) or as a single composite genre label.

- missing_facts_or_bridge:
  - Explicit genre list for Heavyweights (film).
  - Clarification on how to decompose "romantic comedy-drama" and whether "Christmas" should count as a genre for the comparison.

- retrieval_direction (priority):
  1. Heavyweights (film) — fetch the Wikipedia page "Heavyweights (film)" or "Heavyweights (1995 film)" to get official genre(s).
  2. If needed, authoritative sources: IMDB or Wikipedia genre section for Heavyweights.
  3. Confirm genre decomposition rule: "romantic comedy-drama" — search "romantic comedy-drama genre decomposition Wikipedia" or check The Santa Clause 2 page for genre tags.
hop2_docs: Heavyweights | Heavyweights is a 1995 American comedy-drama film directed by Steven Brill and written by Brill with Judd Apatow.  The film centers around a fat camp for kids that is taken over by a fitness guru named Tony Perkis (Ben Stiller).
Genre (animated film) | Genre is a 1996 Live-action/animated short film by animator Don Hertzfeldt, his second student film, preceded by Ah, L'Amour (1995).

Tyson (1995 film) | Tyson is a 1995 American television film based on the life of American heavyweight boxer Mike Tyson.  Directed by Uli Edel, it is an adaptation of the 1989 book "Fire and Fear: The Inside Story of Mike Tyson" by José Torres, former boxer and former chairman of the New York State Athletic Commission.  The film depicts events from Tyson's troubled childhood in Brooklyn through his conviction in 1992 for the rape of beauty pageant contestant Desiree Washington.

IPWA Heavyweight Championship | The IPWA Heavyweight Championship was a professional wrestling heavyweight championship in the Independent Professional Wrestling Alliance (IPWA).  The inaugural champion was Cueball Carmichael, who defeated Justin St. John following a battle royal on October 9, 1995 to become the first IPWA Heavyweight Champion.

Tom Hodges (actor) | Thomas E. "Tom" Hodges (born July 1, 1965) is an American actor and film producer, famous for roles such as Bruno in the 1986 teen film "Lucas", and Rich in the NBC (later CBS) series "The Hogan Family".  He also appeared in such films as 1987's "", 1988's "", 1989's "Going Overboard" and "Steel Magnolias" and 1995's "Heavyweights".  Hodges was also in the movie "Look".

Lawrence Clay-Bey | Lawrence Marvin Clay-Bey (born December 14, 1965) is an American former professional boxer who fought at heavyweight.  As an amateur he was a two-time winner (1995 and 1996) of the United States National Championships as well as a bronze medalist at the 1995 World Championships, all in the super heavyweight division.

He Ping | He Ping () (born 1957 in Shanxi, China) is a Chinese film director, screenwriter and producer whose main filmography consists of a hybrid genre of Western-wuxia movies.  He made three movies along this genre - "Swordsmen in Double Flag Town" (1991), "Sun Valley" (1995) and "Warriors of Heaven and Earth" (2004).
response.summary_2: They both are comedy-drama films (share the comedy and drama genres). Heavyweights is a 1995 American comedy-drama film. The Santa Clause 2 is a 2002 American Christmas romantic comedy-drama film.
